elements.sent-2411-001: A clear and well-documented \LaTeX\ document is presented as an article formatted for publication by ACM in a conference proceedings or journal publication.
elements.sent-2411-002: Based on the ``acmart'' document class, this article presents and explains many of the common variations, as well as many of the formatting elements an author may use in the preparation of the documentation of their work.
elements.sent-2411-004: ACM's consolidated article template, introduced in 2017, provides a consistent \LaTeX\ style for use across ACM publications, and incorporates accessibility and metadata-extraction functionality necessary for future Digital Library endeavors.
elements.sent-2411-005: Numerous ACM and SIG-specific \LaTeX\ templates have been examined, and their unique features incorporated into this single new template.
elements.sent-2411-006: If you are new to publishing with ACM, this document is a valuable guide to the process of preparing your work for publication.
elements.sent-2411-007: If you have published with ACM before, this document provides insight and instruction into more recent changes to the article template.
elements.sent-2411-008: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare articles for any ACM publication --- conference or journal, and for any stage of publication, from review to final ``camera-ready'' copy, to the author's own version, with { very few changes to the source.}
elements.sent-2411-009: As noted in the introduction, the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare many different kinds of documentation --- a double-anonymous initial submission of a full-length technical paper, a two-page SIGGRAPH Emerging Technologies abstract, a ``camera-ready'' journal article, a SIGCHI Extended Abstract, and more --- all by selecting the appropriate { template style and { template parameters .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This document will explain the major features of the document class.
elements.sent-2411-011: For further information, the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide is available from \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The primary parameter given to the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class is the { template style which corresponds to the kind of publication or SIG publishing the work.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This parameter is enclosed in square brackets and is a part of the {\verb|documentclass| command:.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Journals use one of three template styles.
elements.sent-2411-015: All but three ACM journals use the {\verb|acmsmall| template style:.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The majority of conference proceedings documentation will use the {\verb|acmconf| template style.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition to specifying the { template style to be used in formatting your work, there are a number of { template parameters which modify some part of the applied template style.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: A complete list of these parameters can be found in the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Frequently-used parameters, or combinations of parameters, include:.
elements.sent-2411-020: Anonymizes the work and includes line numbers.
elements.sent-2411-021: Use with the \acmSubmissionID command to print the submission's unique ID on each page of the work.
elements.sent-2411-022: This document uses the following string as the first command in the source file:.
elements.sent-2411-023: Modifying the template --- including but not limited to: adjusting margins, typeface sizes, line spacing, paragraph and list definitions, and the use of the \verb|| command to manually adjust the vertical spacing between elements of your work --- is not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-024: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class requires the use of the ``Libertine'' typeface family.
elements.sent-2411-025: Your \TeX\ installation should include this set of packages.
elements.sent-2411-026: Please do not substitute other typefaces.
elements.sent-2411-027: The ``\verb|lmodern|'' and ``\verb|ltimes|'' packages should not be used, as they will override the built-in typeface families.
elements.sent-2411-028: The title of your work should use capital letters appropriately - \url{https://capitalizemytitle.com/ has useful rules for capitalization.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Use the {\verb|title| command to define the title of your work.}
elements.sent-2411-030: If your work has a subtitle, define it with the {\verb|subtitle| command.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Do not insert line breaks in your title.
elements.sent-2411-032: If your title is lengthy, you must define a short version to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-033: The \verb|title| command has a ``short title'' parameter:.
elements.sent-2411-034: Each author must be defined separately for accurate metadata identification.
elements.sent-2411-035: As an exception, multiple authors may share one affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-036: Authors' names should not be abbreviated; use full first names wherever possible.
elements.sent-2411-037: Include authors' e-mail addresses whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-038: Grouping authors' names or e-mail addresses, or providing an ``e-mail alias,'' as shown below, is not acceptable:.
elements.sent-2411-039: The \verb|authornote| and \verb|authornotemark| commands allow a note to apply to multiple authors --- for example, if the first two authors of an article contributed equally to the work.
elements.sent-2411-040: If your author list is lengthy, you must define a shortened version of the list of authors to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-041: The following command should be placed just after the last \verb|| definition:.
elements.sent-2411-042: Omitting this command will force the use of a concatenated list of all of the authors' names, which may result in overlapping text in the page headers.
elements.sent-2411-043: The article template's documentation, available at \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template , has a complete explanation of these commands and tips for their effective use.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that authors' addresses are mandatory for journal articles.
elements.sent-2411-045: Authors of any work published by ACM will need to complete a rights form.
elements.sent-2411-046: Depending on the kind of work, and the rights management choice made by the author, this may be copyright transfer, permission, license, or an OA (open access) agreement.
elements.sent-2411-047: Regardless of the rights management choice, the author will receive a copy of the completed rights form once it has been submitted.
elements.sent-2411-048: This form contains \LaTeX\ commands that must be copied into the source document.
elements.sent-2411-049: When the document source is compiled, these commands and their parameters add formatted text to several areas of the final document:.
elements.sent-2411-050: Rights information is unique to the work; if you are preparing several works for an event, make sure to use the correct set of commands with each of the works.
elements.sent-2411-051: The ACM Reference Format text is required for all articles over one page in length, and is optional for one-page articles (abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-052: Two elements of the ``acmart'' document class provide powerful taxonomic tools for you to help readers find your work in an online search.
elements.sent-2411-053: The ACM Computing Classification System --- \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/class-2012 --- is a set of classifiers and concepts that describe the computing discipline.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Authors can select entries from this classification system, via \url{https://dl.acm.org/ccs/ccs.cfm , and generate the commands to be included in the \LaTeX\ source.}
elements.sent-2411-055: User-defined keywords are a comma-separated list of words and phrases of the authors' choosing, providing a more flexible way of describing the research being presented.
elements.sent-2411-056: CCS concepts and user-defined keywords are required for for all articles over two pages in length, and are optional for one- and two-page articles (or abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-057: Your work should use standard \LaTeX\ sectioning commands: \verb|section|, \verb|subsection|, \verb|subsubsection|, and \verb|paragraph|.
elements.sent-2411-058: They should be numbered; do not remove the numbering from the commands.
elements.sent-2411-059: Simulating a sectioning command by setting the first word or words of a paragraph in boldface or italicized text is { not allowed.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class includes the ``\verb|booktabs|'' package --- \url{https://ctan.org/pkg/booktabs --- for preparing high-quality tables.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Table captions are placed { above the table.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Because tables cannot be split across pages, the best placement for them is typically the top of the page nearest their initial cite.
elements.sent-2411-063: To ensure this proper ``floating'' placement of tables, use the environment table to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-064: The contents of the table itself must go in the tabular environment, to be aligned properly in rows and columns, with the desired horizontal and vertical rules.
elements.sent-2411-065: Again, detailed instructions on tabular material are found in the \LaTeX\ User's Guide.
elements.sent-2411-066: Immediately following this sentence is the point at which Table~\ref{tab:freq is included in the input file; compare the placement of the table here with the table in the printed output of this document.}

elements.sent-2411-067: To set a wider table, which takes up the whole width of the page's live area, use the environment table* to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-068: As with a single-column table, this wide table will ``float'' to a location deemed more desirable.

elements.sent-2411-070: Always use midrule to separate table header rows from data rows, and use it only for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-071: This enables assistive technologies to recognise table headers and support their users in navigating tables more easily.
elements.sent-2411-072: You may want to display math equations in three distinct styles: inline, numbered or non-numbered display.
elements.sent-2411-073: Each of the three are discussed in the next sections.
elements.sent-2411-074: A formula that appears in the running text is called an inline or in-text formula.
elements.sent-2411-075: It is produced by the math environment, which can be invoked with the usual {\char'134begin\,\ldots{\char'134 end construction or with the short form \$\,\ldots\$.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: You can use any of the symbols and structures, from $\alpha$ to $\omega$, available in \LaTeX~\cite{Lamport:LaTeX ; this section will simply show a few examples of in-text equations in context.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Notice how this equation: \begin{math \lim_{n\rightarrow \infty x=0 \end{math , set here in in-line math style, looks slightly different when set in display style.. (See next section).}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: A numbered display equation---one set off by vertical space from the text and centered horizontally---is produced by the equation environment.
elements.sent-2411-079: An unnumbered display equation is produced by the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-080: Again, in either environment, you can use any of the symbols and structures available in \LaTeX\@; this section will just give a couple of examples of display equations in context.
elements.sent-2411-081: First, consider the equation, shown as an inline equation above: \begin{equation}\lim_{n\rightarrow \infty}x=0\end{equation} Notice how it is formatted somewhat differently in the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-082: Now, we'll enter an unnumbered equation: \begin{displaymath \sum_{i=0 ^{\infty x + 1 \end{displaymath and follow it with another numbered equation: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}x_i=\int_{0}^{\pi+2} f\end{equation} just to demonstrate \LaTeX's able handling of numbering.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The ``\verb|figure|'' environment should be used for figures.
elements.sent-2411-084: One or more images can be placed within a figure.
elements.sent-2411-085: If your figure contains third-party material, you must clearly identify it as such, as shown in the example below.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{sample-franklin} \caption{1907 Franklin Model D roadster. Photograph by Harris \& Ewing, Inc. [Public domain], via Wikimedia Commons. (\url{https://goo.gl/VLCRBB}).} \Description{A woman and a girl in white dresses sit in an open car.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Your figures should contain a caption which describes the figure to the reader.
elements.sent-2411-087: Figure captions are placed { below the figure.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Every figure should also have a figure description unless it is purely decorative.
elements.sent-2411-089: These descriptions convey what’s in the image to someone who cannot see it.
elements.sent-2411-090: They are also used by search engine crawlers for indexing images, and when images cannot be loaded.
elements.sent-2411-091: A figure description must be unformatted plain text less than 2000 characters long (including spaces).
elements.sent-2411-092: For figures that convey important and complex new information, a short text description may not be adequate.
elements.sent-2411-093: More complex alternative descriptions can be placed in an appendix and referenced in a short figure description.
elements.sent-2411-094: For example, provide a data table capturing the information in a bar chart, or a structured list representing a graph.
elements.sent-2411-095: For additional information regarding how best to write figure descriptions and why doing this is so important, please see \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/taps/describing-figures/ .}
elements.sent-2411-096: A ``teaser figure'' is an image, or set of images in one figure, that are placed after all author and affiliation information, and before the body of the article, spanning the page.
elements.sent-2411-097: If you wish to have such a figure in your article, place the command immediately before the \verb|| command:.

elements.sent-2411-119: Basé sur la classe de document ``acmart'', ce l'article présente et explique de nombreuses variations courantes, ainsi que autant d'éléments de mise en forme qu'un auteur peut utiliser dans le préparation de la documentation de leur travail.
elements.sent-2411-120: Basierend auf der Dokumentenklasse ``acmart'' präsentiert und erklärt dieser Artikel viele der Formatierungselemente sowie auch viele der gängigen Variationen, die ein Autor bei der Beschreibung seiner Arbeit verwenden darf.
elements.sent-2411-121: Basado en la clase de documento ``acmart'', este artículo presenta y explica muchas de las variaciones comunes, así como tantos de los elementos de formato que un autor puede usar en el preparación de la documentación de su trabajo.
elements.sent-2411-122: The use of \BibTeX\ for the preparation and formatting of one's references is strongly recommended.
elements.sent-2411-123: Authors' names should be complete --- use full first names (``Donald E.
elements.sent-2411-125: Knuth'') --- and the salient identifying features of a reference should be included: title, year, volume, number, pages, article DOI, etc. The bibliography is included in your source document with these two commands, placed just before the \verb|.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this paper we present an approach to reduce hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating Knowledge Graphs (KGs) as an additional modality.
elements.sent-2411-127: Our method involves transforming input text into a set of KG embeddings and using an adapter to integrate these embeddings into the language model space, without relying on external retrieval processes.
elements.sent-2411-128: To facilitate this, we created WikiEntities, a dataset containing over 3 million Wikipedia texts annotated with entities from Wikidata and their corresponding embeddings from PyTorch-BigGraph.
elements.sent-2411-129: This dataset serves as a valuable resource for training Entity Linking models and adapting the described method to various LLMs using specialized adapters.
elements.sent-2411-130: Our method does not require fine-tuning of the language models themselves; instead, we only train the adapter.
elements.sent-2411-131: This ensures that the model's performance on other tasks is not affected.
elements.sent-2411-132: We trained an adapter for the Mistral 7B, LLaMA 2-7B (chat), and LLaMA 3-8B (instruct) models using this dataset and demonstrated that our approach improves performance on the HaluEval, True-False benchmarks and FEVER dataset.
elements.sent-2411-133: The results indicate that incorporating KGs as a new modality can effectively reduce hallucinations and improve the factual accuracy of language models, all without the need for external retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-134: The code, models and datasets will be publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-135: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving technologies in modern artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-136: Despite advancements in training schemes such as RLHF~\cite{kaufmann2024survey and the resulting significant improvement in conversational skills~\cite{ouyang2022training , these models still face the problem of hallucinations - the factual inaccuracy of the generated text~\cite{huang2023survey .}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: In this work, we propose a method to reduce hallucinations in LLMs by incorporating KG as an additional modality.
elements.sent-2411-138: Our approach involves predicting KG embeddings based on the input text and using an adapter to integrate these embeddings into the language model space without relying on external retrieval processes.
elements.sent-2411-139: This process is depicted in the Figure~\ref{fig:graph_as_a_modality .}
elements.sent-2411-140: We introduce WikiEntities — a dataset of over 3 million Wikipedia texts annotated with entities from Wikidata~\cite{10.1145/2629489 and their corresponding embeddings from PyTorch-BigGraph~\cite{pbg .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: This dataset is designed to train a mapping model that maps contextual text embeddings to KGs embeddings and an LLM adapter that integrates these embeddings into the input of the given Language Model.
elements.sent-2411-142: Our proposed method can be adapted to any model by simply training a specific mapper from KG embeddings to the embeddings of the desired model, ensuring a straightforward implementation.
elements.sent-2411-143: By applying this setup for Mistral-7B~\cite{jiang2023mistral , LLaMA 2-7B~\cite{touvron2023llama , and LLaMA 3-8B~\cite{llama3modelcard , we demonstrate that it improves the models' performance in hallucination reduction and hallucination detection, providing more accurate responses to user queries and maintaining quality of other tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Thus, the contribution of this paper is twofold:.
elements.sent-2411-145: We present WikiEntities — a dataset of over 3 million texts from Wikipedia annotated with entities, entity identifiers, and their embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-146: This dataset can be used to train Entity Linking models and it can also extend the described method to any large language model by training specialized adapters for specific models.
elements.sent-2411-147: We add KG information as an additional modality to the Mistral 7B, LLaMA 2-7B, and LLaMA 3-8B models and show that this approach reduces the model hallucinations while maintaining the same performance on other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-148: LLMs often suffer from high levels of hallucinations and lack interpretability in problem solving \cite{Golovneva2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: To improve the reliability and factual accuracy of language model responses, several approaches have been proposed, covering various aspects of language modeling and data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-150: The work by \citet{xu-etal-2023-fine involves fine-tuning LLMs for high-quality Wikidata-related questions and answers \citet{Huo_2023 investigates a method for automatically verifying LLM responses using a corpus.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Another study \cite{sun-etal-2023-towards expands the training set with contrastive samples exhibiting different degrees of errors.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Another branch of research focuses on incorporating additional knowledge into the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-153: While retrieved documents or KG entities are most commonly represented as text, an alternative approach is to represent an additional modality in latent space, which has recently shown promising results for various modalities, from images to audio.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figs/kg_embs_vika.drawio.pdf} \caption{The Proposed Pipeline Scheme. The input text is tokenized into LLM input and forms an additional KG modality by passing through Text2Graph mapper and Adapter, which is added to input sequence.} \label{fig:graph_as_a_modality}
elements.sent-2411-154: Incorporating additional modalities into pretrained LLMs using adapters has shown promising results in various domains.
elements.sent-2411-155: One of the first attempts in this direction was the integration of the visual modality into language models using a simple MLP block, as proposed in FROMAGe \cite{KohSF23 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: In this work, the authors introduced a single embedding that compresses the visual encoder input and projects it into the language model space.
elements.sent-2411-157: The results demonstrated a high level of adaptation, allowing the model to translate information from the visual modality into the language model space and even function as a dialog agent operating simultaneously with both modalities.
elements.sent-2411-158: Subsequent studies have built upon this technique by increasing the number of additional tokens representing modalities and employing various encoders for more enriched modality representations \cite{LiuLWL23a,liu2024improved,goncharova2024omnifusion,InternVL2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Additionally, more complex adapters such as QFormer have been developed to further improve the integration of these modalities \cite{zhong2024enhancing .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Apart from visual modalities, other types such as audio and video have also been successfully adapted using similar techniques, as seen in AudioChatLLaMA \cite{fathullah2024audiochatllama and other works \cite{Tang2023,Lin2023,li2024topa .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Furthermore, \citet{kale-etal-2023-kgvl has explored the use of KG in vLM, demonstrating promising results in specific domains such as radiology reporting.}
elements.sent-2411-162: To improve the factual accuracy of Large Language Models~(LLMs), we integrate Knowledge Graph~(KG) information as an additional modality.
elements.sent-2411-163: The structure of our pipeline, illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:graph_as_a_modality , consists of three components: the LLM~(blue), Text2Graph mapper~(yellow), which provides linking between the input text and KG embedding space and a Linear Layer acting as a pre-trained Adapter to convert KG embeddings into LLM embeddings~(red).}
elements.sent-2411-164: The INPUT TEXT~(shown in the bottom left of the figure) is tokenized into the LLM embedding space while simultaneously being processed by the Text2Graph mapper.
elements.sent-2411-165: Text2Graph produces KG embeddings, which are then converted into LLM embeddings using a pre-trained Adapter.
elements.sent-2411-166: This additional modality is encapsulated with special tokens <GRAPH\_START> and <GRAPH\_END>, which are integrated into the input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-167: Based on this input, the LLM generates the OUTPUT TEXT.
elements.sent-2411-168: Our approach eliminates the need to fine-tune the LLM itself; instead, we focus on training the Text2Graph module and the embedding Adapter.
elements.sent-2411-169: These two components of the pipeline are trained independently.
elements.sent-2411-170: Text2Graph is responsible for converting text into the selected KG embedding, aligning with the chosen KG embedding.
elements.sent-2411-171: The Adapter, which interacts with the LLM, needs to be trained separately for each distinct LLM.
elements.sent-2411-172: Training Text2Graph mapper and embedding Adapter requires a large dataset of simple texts enriched with KG entities with its corresponding positions.
elements.sent-2411-173: The existing data sets are small~\cite{hoffart-etal-2011-robust or consist mainly of questions and answers~\cite{yang-etal-2018-hotpotqa,dubey2017lc2,joshi-etal-2017-triviaqa .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: We generated required dataset using a dump of Wikipedia articles.
elements.subsection-2411-008: WikiEntities — Texts Enriched with Wikidata Entities
elements.sent-2411-175: We begin with parsing the Wikipedia dump\footnote{https://dumps.wikimedia.org/enwiki/ , processing the pages sequentially.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Each page is handled using the PlainTextWikipedia\footnote{https://github.com/daveshap/PlainTextWikipedia module, which cleans the text of HTML tags and identifies links to related Wikipedia pages.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The Wikimapper tool\footnote{https://github.com/jcklie/wikimapper is then used to map these pages to their corresponding Wikidata IDs.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Since links can correspond to various Wikidata issues, including categories or other non-entity pages, links that do not match any Wikidata entity are excluded from our data.
elements.sent-2411-179: As a result, processing a single HTML page yields the text of a Wikipedia article with a set of mentioned Wikidata entities and their corresponding positions.
elements.sent-2411-180: An example of this dataset object is shown in Fig. 2.
elements.sent-2411-181: Under United States patent law, the abstract may be called "Abstract of the Disclosure".
elements.sent-2411-182: United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) web site.
elements.sent-2411-183: It contains text with Wikidata entities in it along with their respective positions.
elements.sent-2411-184: This process was applied to the entire Wikipedia dump, resulting in a dataset of over 3.2 million Wikipedia texts, each enriched with corresponding Wikidata entities.
elements.sent-2411-185: Each text is annotated with the entities occurring in the text along with their positions.
elements.sent-2411-186: The corresponding graph embeddings for these entities are collected in a separate lookup table, allowing for their efficient retrieval and integration into our models.
elements.sent-2411-187: This dataset serves as the main resource for training our Text2Graph embedding mapper and KG embedding Adapters.
elements.sent-2411-188: Additionally, it can be highly beneficial for entity linking tasks, both for training and evaluation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-189: The associated statistics of the dataset are in Table~\ref{table_datasets_stat .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{WikiEntities Dataset Statistics} \begin{tabular}{|l | c|} Dataset Full Number of text $3,276,930$ \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Lengths of texts (words)} Avg $4,526$ Maximum $948,290$ Minimum $3$ \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Number of unique entities per text} Avg $19$ Maximum $7,146$ Minimum $0$ \end{tabular} \label{table_datasets_stat}
elements.sent-2411-190: The Text2Graph Mapper links text with specific KG embeddings, requiring retraining only if the KG embedding space changes.
elements.sent-2411-191: In our study, we use TransE~\cite{NIPS2013_1cecc7a7 KG representation.}
elements.sent-2411-192: We use RoBERTa-large~\cite{liu2019roberta to develop our Text2Graph encoder, which converts text spans containing Wikidata entities into KG.}
elements.sent-2411-193: This encoder comprises the RoBERTa-large model, with all weights unfrozen, and an additional linear layer designed specifically to predict graph embeddings from the encoded text representations.
elements.sent-2411-194: We split each text from the WikiEntities dataset into spans containing one entity surrounded by 20 tokens of text.
elements.sent-2411-195: These spans are fed into RoBERTa, and the resulting representations are passed through an additional linear layer to predict the KG embedding.
elements.sent-2411-196: The training process employs Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss to minimize the difference between the predicted and actual Wikidata embeddings for the given entity.
elements.sent-2411-197: Training is performed using AdamW optimiser for 1 epoch with Learning Rate $1{\rm e ^{-4}$ and Weight Decay ${1\rm e ^{-3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: We train a single linear layer Adapter using the WikiEntities dataset.
elements.sent-2411-199: For training objective, we use a standard language modeling task, with a cross-entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-200: Text2Graph is not involved in this process; as an additional KG modality embedding, we consider Wikidata entities corresponding to the text.
elements.sent-2411-201: Training is performed using AdamW optimizer in combination with a Cosine Scheduler during 1 epoch with Learning Rate $5{\rm e ^{-3}$ and Weight Decay ${1\rm e ^{-3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: The LLM itself remains frozen during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-203: Additionally, we introduce two special tokens: <GRAPH\_START> and <GRAPH\_END>, both with trainable embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-204: What is the largest ocean liner in the world? The largest ocean liners in the world are the RMS Titanic Mistral + KG: "Imperator" is the largest ship in the Imperator class ocean liners.
elements.sent-2411-205: It was built in 1913 by the Harland and Wolff shipyard in Belfast, Northern Ireland, for the Hamburg America Line.
elements.sent-2411-206: Does Europium appear in its liquid state? No, Europium does not appear in its standard liquid state.
elements.sent-2411-207: Mistral + KG: No. Europium appears in its standard state as a solid.
elements.sent-2411-208: Europium is a silvery-white metal that is soft enough to be cut with a knife.
elements.sent-2411-209: It has a melting point of 1529 C.

elements.sent-2411-210: We evaluate Mistral-$7B$-v$0.1$, LLaMA $2-7B$~(chat), and LLaMA $3-8B$~(instruct) models with and without the additional KG modality, measuring their ability to recognize and reduce hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-211: In the Hallucination Detection setup, the model receives a prompt along with a set of tasks, such as summarization, question answering, fact checking, and corresponding answers.
elements.sent-2411-212: The model's assignment is to is to determine whether any hallucinations are present in the provided answers.
elements.sent-2411-213: We validate our approach on two primary benchmarks - HaluEval~\cite{HaluEval and True-False~\cite{azaria2023internal - and Fever~\cite{Thorne18Fever dataset.}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: We conducted two types of experiments:.
elements.sent-2411-215: Detailed descriptions of the benchmark experiments are provided in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-216: Additionally, we engaged the models with questions based on factual information from Wikipedia.
elements.sent-2411-217: Examples of their responses are shown in Fig. 3, which demonstrates that incorporating an additional KG modality enhances the model's ability to answer fact-based questions.
elements.sent-2411-218: The Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models (HaluEval)~\cite{HaluEval includes three tasks:.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Question Answering~(QA) consists of questions and corresponding answers and based on $10\,000$ randomly selected samples from the HotPotQA~\cite{yang-etal-2018-hotpotqa dataset;.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Dialogue consists of questions, answers and dialoque history.
elements.sent-2411-221: It based on $10\,000$ samples from OpenDialKG~\cite{moon-etal-2019-opendialkg - a dataset of conversations between two crowdsourcing agents;.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Summarization based on $10\,000$ samples from CNN/Daily Mail~\cite{see-etal-2017-get datasets and contains texts and its summary.}
elements.sent-2411-223: In this benchmark, hallucinated responses are generated using ChatGPT configured with instructions specifically designed to induce hallucinations, while real responses from datasets serve as references.
elements.sent-2411-224: We utilize a model harnessing approach akin to the method depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:graph_as_a_modality .}
elements.sent-2411-225: Our input consists of a text comprising a prompt, a question, and an answer (or, for summarization tasks, a prompt, text, and summary).
elements.sent-2411-226: The model's output is expected to identify whether hallucinations are present.
elements.sent-2411-227: Prompts for each task are obtained from the HaluEVal repository~\footnote{\href{https://github.com/RUCAIBox/HaluEval?tab=readme-ov-file {HaluEVal repository .}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: For the QA task, Text2Graph maps only the question to the KG embedding.
elements.sent-2411-229: In Dialogue tasks, it maps the dialogue history.
elements.sent-2411-230: For summarization, the entire text is processed by Text2Graph.
elements.sent-2411-231: Given that the text for summarization often exceeded Roberta's context length, we divided it into chunks matching the context length and performed the KG embedding procedure for each chunk.
elements.sent-2411-232: Consequently, the sequence of KG embeddings for summarization consisted of multiple elements rather than just one.
elements.sent-2411-233: The QA dataset is derived from HotPotQA, where each question-answer pair is linked to entities from the KG.
elements.sent-2411-234: For this dataset, we use the provided entity embeddings rather than generating them with Text2Graph.
elements.sent-2411-235: This option is indicated as ``real KG embs'' in the resulting table.
elements.sent-2411-236: The results for all three tasks across different configurations are summarized in Table~\ref{table_Halu_eval .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Results for model with and without KG modality for FEVER dataset.} 0.99{ \begin{tabular}{|l | c | } Model Result Mistral 7B 0.665 Mistral 7B + KG 0.743 LLaMA 2-7B 0.693 LLaMA 2-7B + KG 0.726 LLaMA 3-8B 0.774 LLaMA 3-8B + KG 0.785 \end{tabular}} \label{fever}
elements.sent-2411-237: The True-False dataset from~\cite{azaria2023internal is divided into eight topics: Cities, Inventions, Elements, Animals, Generated, Facts, and Companies.}
elements.sent-2411-238: It consists of statements that need to be evaluated for reliability.
elements.sent-2411-239: We conducted an experiment akin to the one described earlier, where both the LLM and Text2Graph receive a given statement to verify its correctness.
elements.sent-2411-240: For this dataset, we employed the 8-shot prompt template recommended on the Hallucinations Leaderboard\footnote{https://hallucinations-leaderboard-leaderboard.hf.space/ .}
elements.sent-2411-241: The prompt is detailed in Appendix~\ref{sec:appendixA .}
elements.sent-2411-242: The overall quality assessment results are summarized in Table~\ref{table_true_false .}
elements.sent-2411-243: FEVER~(Fact Extraction and VERification)~\cite{Thorne18Fever comprises $185,445$ claims created by modifying sentences taken from Wikipedia.}
elements.sent-2411-244: To evaluate their correctness, we used the scheme outlined in Sec.~\ref{sec:truefalse .}
elements.sent-2411-245: The prompt for this dataset is available in Appendix~\ref{sec:appendixA , and the results are presented in Table~\ref{fever .}}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Evaluation results on benchmarks for KG-based and original Mistral-$7$B-v$0.1$, LLaMA 2-7B~(chat), and LLaMA 3-8B~(instruct) models.} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Model \shortstack{MMLU (avg acc)} \shortstack{GSM8k (acc)} \shortstack{TruthfulQA (mc2)} \shortstack{Winogrande (acc)} \shortstack{Hellaswag (acc\_norm)} \shortstack{ARC (acc\_norm)} Mistral 7B 0.623 0.378 0.422 0.784 0.785 0.613 Mistral 7B + predicted KG embs 0.618 0.369 0.414 0.789 0.830 0.616 LLaMA 2-7B 0.474 0.223 0.453 0.740 0.786 0.532 LLaMA 2-7B + predicted KG embs 0.475 0.224 0.450 0.739 0.782 0.537 LLaMA 3-8B 0.657 0.757 0.517 0.776 0.789 0.611 LLaMA 3-8B + predicted KG embs 0.657 0.738 0.523 0.782 0.785 0.613 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:benchresults}
elements.sent-2411-246: We further tested our method on standard language understanding and reasoning benchmarks to ensure that integrating KGs does not negatively impact the model performance on other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-247: The models were evaluated on six commonly used benchmarks, including MMLU~\cite{eval-harness , GSM8k~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2110-14168 , TruthfulQA~\cite{lin-etal-2022-truthfulqa , Winogrande~\cite{sakaguchi2021winogrande , HellaSwag~\cite{zellers2019hellaswag , and ARC~\cite{clark2018arc .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:benchresults .}
elements.sent-2411-249: We used the Eleuther AI Language Model Evaluation Harness framework~\cite{eval-harness to evaluate our model across six commonly-used benchmarks:.}
elements.sent-2411-250: AI2 Reasoning Challenge~(25-shot): Grade-school level science question set (log-likelihood);.
elements.sent-2411-252: MMLU~(5-shot): Multiple-choice questions assessing the model's ability to solve college-level tasks in a variety of scientific disciplines (log-likelihood);.
elements.sent-2411-253: TruthfulQA~(6-shot): A test measuring the model's tendency to reproduce false information commonly found online (log-likelihood);.
elements.sent-2411-254: Winogrande~(5-shot): The Winograd benchmark for commonsense reasoning (log-likelihood);.
elements.sent-2411-255: GSM8k~(5-shot): Grade school math word problems that assess the model's multi-step mathematical reasoning abilities (generative).
elements.sent-2411-256: The number of shots in brackets indicates the number of examples shown to the model before presenting the final question.
elements.sent-2411-257: In Table \ref{tab:benchresults we show the results of the evaluation on the above benchmarks for two versions of the models: with (+KG) and without injection of KG.}
elements.sent-2411-258: In the HaluEval benchmark, LLaMA 2-7B with KG showed the most significant improvement, achieving an increase of approximately 10\% in both the QA and Dialogue tasks.
elements.sent-2411-259: LLaMA3-8B suceeds on the summarization task, showing an improvement of approximately 4\%.
elements.sent-2411-260: Although LLaMA 3's performance on the QA task was not as good as the other models, it excelled on the Dialogue task, outperforming the other models by 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-261: This discrepancy may be related to the nature of the questions: QA primarily features questions that require precise answers (``Which tennis player won more Grand Slam titles, Henri Leconte or Jonathan Stark?''), whereas the Dialogue task involves more abstract questions (``Do you have any thing similar to the story Who Moved My Cheese?'').
elements.sent-2411-262: Mistral showed a consistent improvement of approximately 1.5-2\% across all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-263: On average, without KG modality this model performs better than LLaMA 2, but slightly worse than LLaMA-3.
elements.sent-2411-264: When adding KG, the performance hierarchy changes: LLaMA3 performs the best, followed by LLaMA2, with Mistral trailing behind.
elements.sent-2411-265: Using provided entity embeddings in QA task performs slightly worse than predicting KG embedding by Text2Graph linker.
elements.sent-2411-266: In the True-False benchmark, including the KG modality also improves the models' ability to recognize false statements in each topic set.
elements.sent-2411-267: The best performance is shown by LLaMA 3-8B, followed by Mistral, and then --- LLaMA 2-7B.
elements.sent-2411-268: The similar case is for FEVER: we get the best growth on Mistral~($\approx8\%$), then LLaMA 2~($\approx3\%$) and LLaMA 3~($\approx1\%$).
elements.sent-2411-269: Datasets from Section~\ref{sec:lu_res are more focused on assessing the LLM's ability to construct relevant reasoning or perform graphical analysis rather than on fact cheking.}
elements.sent-2411-270: For example, questions like ``When will the production frontier be a straight line?'' (MMLU) and ``What color is the sun when viewed from space?'' (TruthfulQA) are used.
elements.sent-2411-271: Such question do not explicitly contain information from Wikipedia, so we did not expect our approach to enhance quality.
elements.sent-2411-272: However, it is important to ensure that adding KG information does not affect language comprehension and reasoning LLM ability.
elements.sent-2411-273: In general, overall results suggest that incorporating KGs as an additional modality is a viable approach to improve the factual accuracy of language models without compromising their performance on other task.
elements.sent-2411-274: We have introduced a method for integrating a KG modality into Large Language Models~(LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-275: The method does not require modifications to the LLM or additional graph structures and retrieval techniques; it only needs a Text2Graph linker and a linear layer as an Adapter.
elements.sent-2411-276: This approach helps reduce hallucinations in language models such as Mistral-7B, Llama2-7B, and Llama3-8B without the performance degradation on other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-277: Additionally, we have developed the WikiEntities dataset, which includes over 3 million annotated texts.
elements.sent-2411-278: This dataset can be helpful for Entity Linking models and it also allows us to adapt our KG integration method to different language models.
elements.sent-2411-279: The source code, link to models and dataset will be available at anonimized repository.\footnote{\url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/kg_reduces_halus-D67B/README.md.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: We used pretrained models from the Hugging Face repository.
elements.sent-2411-281: To avoid potential harm or any ethical issues, we exclusively used open-source datasets and publicly available source code.
elements.sent-2411-282: By prioritizing ethical standards and acknowledging potential risks, we aim to promote responsible and sustainable research practice.
elements.sent-2411-283: Given a statement, your objective is to determine if the provided statement correct or not.
elements.sent-2411-284: Write "True" if the given statement is true and "False" if given statement is false.
elements.sent-2411-285: The answer you give MUST be "True" or "False".
elements.sent-2411-286: For example: Statement: Marlon Brando refused to be in any films in 1972.
elements.sent-2411-287: Your Judgement: "False" Statement: Dracula is a written work.
elements.sent-2411-288: Your Judgement: "True" Statement: Maria Sharapova passed all drug tests at the 2016 Australian Open.
elements.sent-2411-289: Your Judgement: "False" Statement: Blood are red.. Your Judgement: "True".
elements.sent-2411-290: Statement: David Cronenberg has a cameo in To Die For.. Your Judgement: "True".
elements.sent-2411-291: Statement: Albert Einstein was born in Moscow.. Your Judgement: "False".
elements.sent-2411-292: Statement: Cubango is a city in Australia.. Your Judgement: "False".
elements.sent-2411-293: Statement: The Okavango River is a river in southwest Africa.. Your Judgement: "True".
elements.sent-2411-294: Write "SUPPORTS" if the given statement is true and "REFUTES" if given statement is false.
elements.sent-2411-295: The answer you give MUST be "SUPPORTS" or "REFUTES".
elements.sent-2411-296: For example: Statement: Furia is adapted from Graffiti and it is French.
elements.sent-2411-297: Your Judgement: "SUPPORTS" Statement: Prince (musician) was not backed by the New Power Generation.
elements.sent-2411-298: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" Statement: Ronin is a 2001 film.
elements.sent-2411-299: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" Statement: The 1998 NFL Draft was cancelled April 18 -- 19.
elements.sent-2411-300: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" Statement: Usher's sophomore single is My Way.
elements.sent-2411-301: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" Statement: Anna Kendrick made her film debut in Camp.
elements.sent-2411-302: Your Judgement: "SUPPORTS" Statement: The G1 Climax is held each March.
elements.sent-2411-303: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" Statement: Loving was directed by Michael Shannon.
elements.sent-2411-304: Your Judgement: "REFUTES" \end{tcolorbox \captionsetup{type=figure \captionof{figure {The default 8-shot prompt for FEVER dataset.}}}}
